# Dugong Field Notes

> A working journal from the team building Dugong, AI-native workflow automation. Essays, post-mortems, and field research on the playbooks that quietly run modern ecommerce and operations teams. Dugong lets you describe a workflow in plain language and compiles it into a running automation.

Site: https://blog.dugong.live
Publisher: Dugong (https://dugong.live)
Topics: workflow automation, AI agents, Shopify automation, ecommerce operations, support-ticket deflection, no-code/natural-language automation.

## Articles

- [The death of the drag-and-drop builder](https://blog.dugong.live/): The site's lead essay (Dispatch Nº 47). For fifteen years workflow automation looked like a canvas of rectangles connected by lines; after watching three million runs across twelve thousand teams, the Dugong team argues the canvas itself was always the problem. Makes the case for describing a workflow in plain language and letting a compiler turn it into running automation with triggers, branching, and integrations — instead of hand-wiring brittle node graphs. By Aanya Rao, 2026-05-28.
- [The sold-out products that sink your collection](https://blog.dugong.live/the-sold-out-products-that-sink-your-collection.html): Out-of-stock merchandising is a long-running, heavily-upvoted Shopify forum complaint everyone assumes is a setting and is not. Shopify has no reliable way to push sold-out products to the bottom of a collection page: some sort options and theme code push out-of-stock down within a single page, but once pagination kicks in each page sorts itself, so a later page can be all sold-out while the first is mixed, and Shopify's Liquid sort filter has no inventory_quantity parameter so theme code cannot sort cleanly across the whole collection either. The only native, reliable option is dragging products by hand in a manual sort, which does not scale past a few small collections. Hiding with a smart-collection "inventory greater than 0" condition is all-or-nothing: it only works on automated collections (not manual ones), does not apply to site search, and removes the product entirely, throwing away its SEO ranking and the back-in-stock signup on the page. The cost is large: stockouts account for roughly 40% of lost sales, nearly 7 in 10 shoppers leave without buying when they cannot find what they came for (often for a competitor), about 51% of products hit at least one stockout a year and stay unavailable ~35 days on average, and the typical out-of-stock rate sits near 8% (climbing to ~10% on promotional items), so at any moment about one tile in twelve is a dead end and it is worse on a sale day. A sold-out product at the top of a collection does not just lose its own sale, it drags down the conversion of every in-stock product behind it. Covers why hand-reordering, smart-collection hiding, and theme code or apps all fail (the real problem is merchandising judgement and freshness, not a one-time sort), and how an AI playbook watches a real inventory change across every collection and search surface, demotes a freshly sold-out product below the in-stock items instead of off the page (keeping its URL and notify-me form alive), keeps items restocking within the week visible with a back-soon badge, protects hero and high-ranking products from being buried, applies per-surface rules (demote on browse, drop from search, leave hand-curated edits to the merchant's rules), restores original positions automatically on restock, and flags a collection that has gone mostly empty. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-13.
- [The restock alert that never fires](https://blog.dugong.live/the-restock-alert-that-never-fires.html): Back-in-stock notification is the Shopify gap everyone assumes is a setting and is not. Shopify has no built-in feature that emails or texts customers when an out-of-stock product is restocked; some themes show a "notify me" form, but it only collects an email into a list and does not watch inventory or send anything, so the standard advice in the forums is to export the addresses and message people by hand. The cost is large and recoverable: stockouts account for roughly 40% of lost sales, the lost sales ratio (revenue missed because items were unavailable) runs about 20 to 30% in ecommerce, about 69% of online shoppers buy from a competitor rather than wait, and a stocked-out product stays unavailable for an average of around 35 days. A back-in-stock request is the warmest possible lead because the shopper has named exactly what they want and given you a way to reach them, yet in most stores the request lands in a void. Covers the three failure modes (the request goes nowhere, a single mass blast sends the whole waitlist at once so the units sell to whoever clicks fastest while everyone else hits a sold-out page, and an alert fired off an unsynced count causes overselling), and why manual emailing, one big announcement, and Shopify Flow or a basic app all fail (the judgement is who to tell, in what order, throttled to real units, on which channel, and when to stop). The AI playbook watches a real (synced) inventory change, pulls the waitlist for that exact variant by wait time, notifies first-requested-first only as many people as there are units, sends SMS or email per the channel the customer chose with a deep link to the variant, throttles the next wave only if more stock lands, closes the list on sellthrough, and escalates hyped drops or tiny restocks to a human before anything sends. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-13.
- [The chargeback you lose by default](https://blog.dugong.live/the-chargeback-you-lose-by-default.html): Chargebacks are the Shopify pain where merchants do the work and still lose, because doing nothing looks almost identical to doing something. With Shopify Payments, Shopify auto-populates the basic transaction data and submits a response to the issuing bank on the deadline date, but it does not read the reason code, match the evidence to it, or write a rebuttal, so the default response is a generic packet filed on the last day. Merchants get a short one-shot window (usually 7–21 days) to add the evidence that actually wins, and once the response is sent or the deadline passes nothing more can be submitted and the bank's decision is final (no appeal). Win rates are low: US merchants win ~54% of disputes they fight through representment, and only ~17% of fraud-coded chargebacks. Friendly fraud (a real customer disputing a real, delivered purchase) is now ~75% of chargebacks and rising at a double-digit annual rate. The all-in cost of a single dispute is ~$315 (avg transaction ~$84) once the fee, lost goods, shipping, and staff time are counted; ecommerce chargebacks are projected at ~$33.8B in 2025. Winning requires reason-code-specific evidence scattered across systems (AVS/CVV match + device/IP for fraud, carrier tracking + signature for item-not-received, listing + accepted refund policy for not-as-described). Covers why leaning on Shopify's auto-response, pre-emptively refunding risky orders, and Shopify Flow all fail (a chargeback is a reason code attached to a story, not a clean trigger), and how an AI playbook reads the reason code, decides if a dispute is winnable or worth conceding, gathers the matching evidence from the processor/carrier/help desk, writes a point-by-point rebuttal, files it well before the deadline, and escalates high-value, ambiguous, or repeat-disputer cases to a human. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-11.
- [The return that won't approve itself](https://blog.dugong.live/the-return-that-wont-approve-itself.html): Returns are the most-complained-about Shopify workflow that scales directly with sales, and Shopify's native handling leaves the hard parts manual. Self-serve returns let a customer request a return but every request still has to be manually approved or declined in the admin (Shopify Flow cannot fully automate approval); true exchanges are not supported (customers can't request one and there are no exchange-specific rules, so a size/colour swap means refunding or cancelling and rebuilding the order by hand, or bolting on an app); return policies can only be set by delivery date and product, not order tags, customer type, or fulfilment date; and refunds go only to the original payment method, so store credit is another manual workaround. Because the refund is the path of least resistance and ~45% of returns are sizing/fit/colour, sales that could have stayed sales walk out as money returned. ~19–20% of online orders are returned (NRF 2025; apparel 20–40%). Returns automation sharply cuts return-related support tickets and speeds processing, and a smooth return experience makes shoppers far more likely to buy again. Covers why "process them faster", manual store-credit nudges, and Shopify Flow all fail (a return request is a natural-language judgement call, not a structured trigger), and how an AI playbook matches the message to the order, checks eligibility, offers an exchange first when stock allows, creates the replacement order and label, settles the price delta, issues credit or refund, confirms in plain language, and escalates only the risky cases (out of window, high-value, repeat returner, damaged item). By Priya Singh, 2026-06-09.
- [The order change that races your warehouse](https://blog.dugong.live/the-order-change-that-races-your-warehouse.html): The post-purchase order-change request ("can you change my order?") is one of the most common and most-complained-about Shopify support tickets. After checkout Shopify gives the customer no self-serve way to fix a typo, swap a size/variant, change quantity, add an item, or update the shipping address; every change routes through the merchant and only while the order is unfulfilled — the moment you click Fulfil items most edits lock, and the billing address can never be edited. So the request becomes a footrace between the customer's email and your own warehouse. ~60–80% of ecommerce tickets are automatable and order changes are among the biggest; ~69% of shoppers prefer self-serve; structured self-serve/automated editing cuts order-change tickets ~40–60%. Covers why "just reply fast", the cancel-and-recreate dance, and Shopify Flow all fail (a change request is a natural-language message, not a structured trigger), and how an AI playbook matches the message to the order, checks fulfilment status, applies safe changes before the lock, settles the price/shipping delta (payment link or refund), confirms in plain language, and escalates only risky cases. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-08.
- [The bad address that ships anyway](https://blog.dugong.live/the-bad-address-that-ships-anyway.html): Bad-address handling is the Shopify pain merchants only notice after it costs them. Shopify runs address validation on every order and shows a yellow warning (incomplete/off) or red error (likely undeliverable), but the flags are advisory — it still lets you fulfil, so during a busy week the warning scrolls past and the package ships to an address that bounces. ~5–10% of packages are returned to sender, with incorrect/incomplete addresses the leading cause; carriers make 1–3 attempts before returning, so a late catch means a return-to-sender fee, a re-ship, a WISMO ticket, and often a refund — many times the original shipping cost. Covers why "watch more carefully" and Flow tag-and-hold both fail (Flow can flag a bad address but can't fix it, and Shopify won't recalc shipping after an edit), and how an AI playbook verifies every address before fulfilment, silently applies confident corrections, emails the customer a suggested fix when ambiguous, and escalates only the unresolvable ones. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-06.
- [The high-risk flag that makes you guess](https://blog.dugong.live/the-high-risk-flag-that-makes-you-guess.html): Shopify's "high-risk" fraud flag is a blackbox that relocates a decision instead of removing it — it scores every order but hands the judgement call back to a merchant who has even less data than the algorithm. Each flagged order is a coin-flip between two expensive mistakes: fulfil and risk a chargeback (avg ~$191, ~$3.75 lost per $1 of fraud, Visa watches at a 0.65% rate), or cancel and risk a false decline (~$443B/yr lost industry-wide vs ~$48B to real fraud; loyal customers wrongly declined cut reorders 65% and 27% never return). Covers why Flow's auto-cancel and tag-and-hold both fail, and how an AI playbook enriches each flagged order with the signals a human would check, auto-decides the clear cases, and escalates only genuine grey areas with a full dossier. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-06.
- [The overselling problem no one automates](https://blog.dugong.live/the-overselling-problem-no-one-automates.html): Overselling — selling stock you no longer have — is one of the most-complained-about Shopify pain points and the hardest to recover from: a missed sale costs a customer you never had, but an oversold order breaks a promise to one you already won. It starts when multiple channels share one stock pool with no real-time sync; ~40% of retailers cancel at least 1 in 10 orders due to inaccurate inventory data. Covers why manual sync, safety buffers, and Flow rules fail during a sale, and how an AI playbook keeps every channel's count honest in real time and catches oversells before fulfilment. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-05.
- [The "where is my order?" tickets that eat your afternoon](https://blog.dugong.live/the-wismo-tickets-that-eat-your-afternoon.html): WISMO ("where is my order?") is the single most common ecommerce support ticket — ~18% of inbox volume on a normal week, over 50% during a sale. Fully-loaded cost is $5–$25 per interaction. Covers why canned auto-replies fail and how an AI playbook answers tracking questions automatically, often before the customer asks. By Priya Singh, 2026-06-04.
- [The Shopify automations no one builds](https://blog.dugong.live/the-shopify-automations-no-one-builds.html): A field study of 1,374 Shopify automation workflows. Tagging is 14% of everything merchants automate; returns and refunds — the work that actually scales with order volume — are under 1%. The gap between what's easy to automate and what's worth automating. By Priya Singh, 2026-05-28.

## About Dugong

- [Dugong product site](https://dugong.live): AI-native automation platform. Describe a playbook in natural language; Dugong's compiler turns it into a running workflow with triggers, branching logic, and integrations.

- [RSS feed](https://blog.dugong.live/feed.xml): Full feed of new dispatches as they publish.
